channel.title: NYT > Style
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Hacky Sack Mounts a Comeback With Gen Z
item.description: Teenagers are booting the game out of the 1990s. “It’s kind of bringing everybody together,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann

item.title: Kurt Vile Finds Inspiration in Philadelphia
item.description: Kurt Vile is finding inspiration in Philadelphia. “The older I get, the more I know every nook and cranny of the city,” he says.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kurt Vile sings the praises of his home city on his new album.

item.title: Did the Pope Really Wear Nikes?
item.description: Eagle-eyed viewers spotted a swoosh in Vatican City.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Carlo Hermann/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Storming Scientology Buildings: TikTok Trend or Hate Crime?
item.description: The police in Los Angeles, New York, Sydney and elsewhere are responding to incidents related to a TikTok trend involving running through Scientology churches.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of teenagers broke a locked door at a Church of Scientology in Midtown Manhattan, the New York Police Department said. It’s part of what has become a global trend.

item.title: Chaos Gardening: A Laid-Back Way to Garden
item.description: Welcome to chaos gardening, a laid-back way to turn a patch of ground into a riot of color.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan

item.title: Thanks to ‘Mormon Wives,’ Dirty Soda Is a National Obsession
item.description: The colorful drinks have become a hit across the country, inspiring new menu items at Dunkin’ and McDonald’s and a genre of TikTok recipes.
item.dc:creator: Gabriella Gershenson
item.category: Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Joe Buglewicz for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Strawberry Breeze, on the menu at a Swig location in Utah. The brand has expanded, growing from 33 locations in 2021 to more than 150 locations now.

item.title: On ‘The Pitt,’ She Clocked Out on Time. In Real Life, It Started a Debate.
item.description: When Joy Kwon, a third-year medical student on TV, set a boundary, she stirred a work-life balance debate among medical professionals and fans.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta

item.title: Hunting For Lexapro Clocks, Viagra Neckties and Other Vintage Pharmaceutical Merch
item.description: The days of giving pharmaceutical-branded swag to health care professionals are largely over. Now those items are hot on the thrifting scene.
item.dc:creator: Chantel Tattoli

item.title: Betty Broderick, Whose Murder Trial Was Grist for TV Movies, Dies at 78
item.description: She killed her ex-husband and his new wife after what she described as years of psychological abuse. The crime inspired TV adaptations, a podcast and at least five books.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: San Diego Union-Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Betty Broderick at the San Diego County Courthouse in 1991, during her second trial for the murder of her ex-husband and his new wife.

item.title: Leigh Magar, High-End Milliner Turned Indigo Artist, Dies at 57
item.description: She was known for bespoke hats that attracted a celebrity following. Then she left Charleston for an island off South Carolina, where she began cultivating indigo.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:credit: Candace Dane Chambers for The New York Times

item.title: Street Style Look of the Week: Mom’s Jacket for a Night on the Town
item.description: A 20-something wearing her mother’s jacket for a Friday evening in Greenwich Village.
item.dc:creator: Simbarashe Cha

item.title: ‘Trimester Zero’: What to Expect When You’re Expecting to Expect
item.description: Influencers and health gurus are offering pregnancy solutions during “trimester zero,” when women are trying to conceive.
item.dc:creator: Kim Hew-Low

item.title: A Taxidermist Gives Dead Animals a New Life
item.description: The creation, care and keeping of creatures is a responsibility the last full-time museum taxidermist in the U.S. takes both seriously and joyfully.
item.dc:creator: Jordan Kisner and Sasha Arutyunova

item.title: What Can I Do About See-Through White Pants?
item.description: A reader loves the style, but hates the consequences.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Bettmann/Getty Images
item.media:description: Jacqueline Kennedy with John Jr. and Caroline on the Greek island of Skorpios on the day before her wedding to Aristotle Onassis.

item.title: A Panorama of Design during the NYCxDesign Festival
item.description: A look at news, products and events during the NYCxDesign Festival.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Joseph Kramm
item.media:description: Chain mail lighting and a tapestry by Loney Abrams and Johnny Stanish, the husband-and-wife duo and founders of Wretched Flowers.

item.title: A Monument to Chocolate Is Wrapped in Layers of Mexican History
item.description: Combining a colonial house and a contemporary addition, the new Museum of Cacao & Chocolate sits on a grisly Aztec souvenir.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Gallow
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Huei, or great Tzompantli. It displays hundreds of human skulls belonging to people who were believed to have been sacrificed in the 15th century.

item.title: Before SoHo, This Building Was at the Heart of New York’s Arts District
item.description: A West Side co-op built for artists and later turned into offices is being reborn as a luxury condominium named Parc Beaufort.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies
item.media:credit: Stefano Ukmar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The model one-bedroom apartment at the Parc Beaufort development on West 57th Street.

item.title: To Jewelry Makers, Titanium Is More Appealing Than Ever
item.description: As gold and silver prices continue to soar, the metal’s relatively inexpensive cost has attracted new users.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Gomelsky
item.media:credit: Beth Coller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Adam Neeley holds up a Morpho earring from Dalí’s Garden Collection.

item.title: Jewelry Among the Exhibits at a Daniel Brush Retrospective
item.description: More than 75 artworks, some described as never displayed in public before, are to go on view June 8 at L’Ecole, School of Jewelry Arts in Paris.
item.dc:creator: Tanya Dukes

item.title: In Jewelry, the Resurgence of Fringe Echoes Freedom of 1920s
item.description: Once a symbol of Jazz Age liberation, fringe now reflects a desire for comfort and joy in jewelry.
item.dc:creator: Milena Lazazzera
item.media:credit: E R Richee/Paramount/Kobal/Shutterstock
item.media:description: The actress Clara Bow in 1927. Her style continues to influence fashion today.

item.title: Hirotaka Inoue’s Jewelry Made the Olympics
item.description: Alysa Liu wore an earring by Hirotaka Inoue during her gold medal-winning skate, sparking new interest in the Japanese designer’s brand.
item.dc:creator: Amy Elliott
item.media:credit: Elsa/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Olympic gold medalist Alysa Liu was spotted wearing an earring design by Hirotaka, a contemporary fine jewelry brand in Tokyo, at the Games in February.

item.title: The Return of the Statement Bangle
item.description: Structured, sculptural bangles and cuffs have proliferated on runways and wrists as alternative metals and natural materials have gained luxe appeal.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Colston

item.title: 5 New Books for Jewelry Lovers
item.description: A menagerie of jewels and family businesses are among the topics of these recent and upcoming releases.
item.dc:creator: Melanie Abrams

item.title: Brooches Now Are Seen Almost Anywhere on Clothing
item.description: The decorative pins are still seen on men’s lapels, but they also may turn up pretty much anywhere on clothing these days.
item.dc:creator: Kathleen Beckett
item.media:credit: Daniel Cole/Reuters
item.media:description: The American singer Shaboozey poses, with brooch, on the red carpet at the 98th Academy Awards in Hollywood.

item.title: A Designer Who Tells Stories Through Her Jewelry
item.description: Ana-Katarina Vinkler-Petrovic says she has spent her career finding beauty in the messy and the complex.
item.dc:creator: Alix Strauss
item.media:credit: Tony Cenicola/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ana-Katarina Vinkler-Petrovic of AnaKatarina Design in Wellesley, Mass.

item.title: ‘Old Woman Naked’ is Pamela Redmond’s Answer to A.I. Concerns
item.description: The writer Pamela Redmond talks about her new play, “Old Woman Naked,” why her kids won’t see it and whether she’d have done the show without GLP-1s.
item.dc:creator: Tammy LaGorce
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Weinberg for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pamela Redmond, the writer of “Younger” has written and is starring in a new play about aging.

item.title: How ‘Mile End Kicks’ Nailed the Indie Sleaze Look
item.description: The film follows a music critic (played by Barbie Ferreira) who moves to Montreal in 2011. Clothes unearthed via Craigslist and vintage piles bring the era to life.
item.dc:creator: Gabriella Gershenson

item.title: A Louisiana Dynasty’s Treasures Come to Market
item.description: Jewelry and art collected by the businesswoman and philanthropist Matilda Geddings Gray and her niece, Matilda Gray Stream, will be offered at auction.
item.dc:creator: Jill Newman

item.title: A Beloved Indie Worrier Is Back, Chiller Than Ever
item.description: The singer-songwriter Courtney Barnett transplanted herself across an ocean to shake things up, not to figure things out.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Lange
item.media:credit: Timothy O’Connell for The New York Times

item.title: Why Everyone Was So Mad About the Met Gala
item.description: Plus, a remembrance of Ted Turner and a tank top quandary.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Beyoncé arriving at the Met Gala on Monday.

item.title: What Is Body Dysmorphic Disorder?
item.description: It may look like vanity, but it’s a debilitating mental health condition.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron

item.title: A Far-Right Experiment in Political Unity
item.description: A gathering of white nationalist candidates and influencers tried to find common ground with the left under the “America First” banner.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Brian Kaiser for The New York Times
item.media:description: On a Saturday afternoon in May, an eclectic group that included Casey Putsch, a Republican running for governor in Ohio, met in an effort to evolve the “America First” movement beyond its de facto leader.

item.title: The Many Love Lives of Ted Turner
item.description: Known as a playboy, the media mogul gave his paramours and three ex-wives plenty of stories to tell. He also managed to stay friendly with many of them.
item.dc:creator: Sadiba Hasan
item.media:credit: Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Jane Fonda, the Academy Award-winning actress, wrote in her memoir that Turner cheated on her a month after they married.

item.title: Writing About My Mother as if She Were Dead
item.description: I needed distance to write honestly about my childhood. But fiction didn’t protect any of us from pain.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Landis
item.media:credit: Brian Rea

item.title: They Met One Night in Queens. A Year in St. Lucia Followed.
item.description: A chance meeting in Queens led Shanae Strachan and Lance Growth to build a life between New York and St. Lucia before marrying in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Donna Kim

item.title: Unmistakable Love of Austin, the Texas Longhorns and Each Other
item.description: Adam Mena and Jesse Stowell brought Austin, Texas, alive at their wedding with a taco truck, burnt orange hues and two miniature shaggy Highland cows.
item.dc:creator: Rosalie R. Radomsky

item.title: She Had Seen Her in Photos. Then They Met in Real Life.
item.description: Nandika Chatterjee had heard about Abigail Braggins from a roommate and thought she was “cute.” A wave in Little Italy sealed their fate.
item.dc:creator: Stella Raine Chu

item.title: A Second Chance Led Them Back to Each Other
item.description: When Matt Sornson and Negarin Sadr first met, they didn’t quite hit it off. But after reconnecting years later online, they built a strong foundation that would later be tested.
item.dc:creator: Sadiba Hasan

item.title: In Cartagena, a Place for Monks and Movie Lovers Is Now a Hotel
item.description: In Cartagena, Colombia, historic buildings, including a monastery and a nightclub, were transformed into a Four Seasons.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi

item.title: This Film Provides a Trapezoidal Window into Former Pizza Huts
item.description: A documentary about the popular chain presents its repurposed buildings as protective shells where new businesses can grow.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Clark Hodgin for The New York Times
item.media:description: The filmmakers Matthew Salleh and Rose Tucker at a Pizza Hut in Newburgh, N.Y. Their documentary “Slice of Life: The American Dream. In Former Pizza Huts.” came out last year.

item.title: A Fashion Revolution at the Met
item.description: With “Costume Art,” the dress department moves out of the basement to become the gateway to the museum.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman and Marcus Maddox
item.media:description: Unexpected bedfellows: The entry room of “Costume Art,” focusing on the naked and nude body, sets the tone for the show, with every garment paired with an accompanying piece from around the museum.

item.title: I’m Unhappily Single. Do I Have to Attend My Friend’s Wedding?
item.description: Our Ask the Therapist columnist, Lori Gottlieb, advises a reader who doesn’t want to be reminded of her loneliness yet again.
item.dc:creator: Lori Gottlieb
item.media:credit: Marta Monteiro

item.title: In ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2,’ Caleb Hearon Gets the Job ‘a Million Girls Would Kill For’
item.description: Caleb Hearon is circling the mainstream with a role in “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” but does his podcast-friendly personality translate into Hollywood stardom?
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caleb Hearon, a comedian whose Midwestern affability draws people in, joined the world of high fashion in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”

item.title: Former Amazon Union Leader Who Was Arrested at Met Gala Has No Regrets
item.description: Christian Smalls, a longtime Amazon antagonist, was charged with trespassing and another misdemeanor, and spent 24 hours in jail. The union disavowed his actions.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
item.media:description: Mr. Smalls had led a successful effort to unionize an Amazon warehouse on Staten Island in 2022.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Buried treasure, birds on the move and secret library access.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Tiny Love Stories: ‘I Could Feel Him Watching Me’
item.description: Modern Love in miniature, featuring reader-submitted stories of no more than 100 words.
item.media:credit: Brian Rea

item.title: A Southern Magazine Now Calls a Historic Charleston Jail Home
item.description: Based in Charleston, S.C., Garden & Gun recently moved to a restored landmark on Magazine Street, of all places.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Hunt and Hunter Mcrae For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Hunter McRae for The New York Times
item.media:description: The “study” area of the new headquarters of Garden & Gun magazine, which moved into Charleston’s Old Jail in March.

item.title: My Mother Won’t Reschedule Her Elective Surgery So I Can Be There. Help!
item.description: A reader wants her mother to pick another date for a hip-replacement surgery so that she doesn’t have to miss an important work conference. Cue the cutting email from Mom.
item.dc:creator: Philip Galanes
item.media:credit: Miguel Porlan

item.title: Having Trouble Choosing the Right White for Your Wedding? This Color Analyst Can Help.
item.description: Megan Bentley, a color analyst, shares how identifying your color season, the hues that are most complementary or flattering, can elevate your wedding wardrobe.
item.dc:creator: Ivy Manners
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Megan Bentley, left, a color analyst and the founder of The Color Countess, based in Columbus, Ohio, works with clients to identify their most flattering color palettes.
